user_prompt: Make a `lib\WifiManager\AsyncFsManager.cpp` and its associated header file
- init, called from any activation of Wi-Fi
- reset walk, reset iterator for file listing, should always work no matter what state
- walk one, return one file from directory, the next call should return the next file, return something to indicate end of list when needed, automatically restart the walk if something is wrong
- open file for download, closes any other file or anything else that might conflict, reset seek pointer
critically, all of these need to be thread safe
web server code can only use methods within this manager, add thread safe functions to this manager as needed
use it to attempt to fix the problem with listing files and the decrypted download
agent_reply: Implemented `AsyncFsManager` and routed the web SD access through it.
What changed:
- Added [AsyncFsManager.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.h:19) and [AsyncFsManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:93).
- It owns the active walk handle and active download handle behind a recursive FreeRTOS mutex.
- `resetWalk`, `walkOne`, `closeWalk`, `openFileForDownload`, `readFileChunk`, and `closeFile` are all serialized.
- Opening a download closes any active walk; resetting a walk closes any active download.
- Added thread-safe helpers for web upload/delete/readiness: `writeFileChunk`, `removeFile`, `isFile`, `isReady`.
I also wired:
- `/list_files.json` to use `AsyncFsManager::resetWalk()` and `walkOne()` instead of holding its own `FsFile`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:221)
- `/download_file` to use `openFileForDownload()` / `readFileChunk()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:413)
- decrypted download to use the manager for encrypted recording reads: [DecryptedDownload.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/DecryptedDownload.cpp:505)
- Wi-Fi activation reset via `AsyncFsManager::init()`: [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:397)
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passes.

user_prompt: it's better, the file list can be retrieved many times
but I attempted a plain file download, it downloaded a mysterious "download_file" file, the size isn't right
and then file listing started to fail
agent_reply: I patched the plain download path.
The important fixes:
- Plain downloads now use a fixed `Content-Length` response instead of open-ended chunked transfer.
- `Content-Disposition` now includes the actual filename, so the browser should save `whatever.rec` instead of `download_file`.
- `AsyncFsManager::readFileChunk()` now clamps reads to the known file size and closes the active download handle at EOF, so a completed download should not poison later `/list_files.json` walks.
- Starting a plain download now closes any active file-list stream first.
Build passes: `pio run -e thefly_sec1`.
This should directly address the mysterious filename, wrong size, and post-download listing failure in one place.

agent_reply: Agreed, and I implemented the first blunt-but-useful version of that coordination.
What changed:
- `AsyncFsManager::guiShouldYield()` now reports when a file walk or download is active.
- `FlyGui::poll()` now returns immediately while the async FS manager is active, so GUI redraw/touch polling won’t compete with SD reads during web listing/download.
- The reopen-and-retry SD read path I added before the interruption is still in place, with SD error code/data logging.
Build passes: `pio run -e thefly_sec1`.

This is intentionally conservative: during a browser file download, the on-device GUI may feel frozen until the transfer ends or disconnects. If this fixes the download corruption, we can refine it into a real shared SPI arbiter or timed “download quiet mode” instead of a broad GUI yield.

commit_message: file download and decrypted downloads work now

user_prompt: I've already made sure 40 MHz SPI doesn't work
I don't see the need for seeking at all, remove it, and I'll test again
agent_reply: Removed steady-state seeking from file downloads.

`AsyncFsManager` now tracks `g_download_position` and only reads forward from the already-open file handle. If the async callback ever asks for a non-sequential position, it logs that and fails instead of silently seeking. The only remaining seek is in the rare reopen-after-error path, so it can resume after a transient SD read failure.
Build passes: `pio run -e thefly_sec1`.
Files touched for this change: [AsyncFsManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:20).

The files now have explicit layout sections, early private prototypes, public/main-flow functions before helper implementations, and supporting/small helper logic moved lower. Behavior should be unchanged.
Verification passed:

- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed.

- `pio run -e thefly_sec2` passed.
`src/version.c` is dirty from the PlatformIO version-generation build step again; I did not manually touch it.

user_prompt: does the web server handlers for "/file_upload" correctly use `lib\WifiManager\AsyncFsManager.cpp` such that the uploads are thread safe?
agent_reply: Short answer: **partly yes, but not completely**.
`/file_upload` does route upload bytes through `AsyncFsManager`:
- Route registration: [WebServer.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:198)
- Body/part callbacks call `write_file_upload_bytes()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:168)
- That calls `AsyncFsManager::writeFileChunk()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:529)

- `writeFileChunk()` takes the recursive FS mutex, closes active walk/download state, opens/writes/syncs/closes the file: [AsyncFsManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:307)
So it is thread-safe **against other code that also goes through `AsyncFsManager`**, such as web file listing/download/delete.
The caveats are the important bit:
1. The lock is per chunk, not per upload. Two simultaneous uploads to the same `file_name` can interleave or truncate each other because each first chunk uses `O_TRUNC`, later chunks use `O_APPEND`.

2. It is not globally thread-safe against other filesystem users. A lot of code uses `MicroSdCard::fs()` / `FsFile` directly, including recording, playback/delete, cloud upload, firmware update, and FTP. Those do not share the `AsyncFsManager` mutex.
So: **the handler correctly uses `AsyncFsManager`, but uploads are only thread-safe within the web/AsyncFsManager lane, not as a whole-device filesystem transaction.**

user_prompt: I got one successful upload, and a few that are partial, ending in a error 500, the file exists on the card but much smaller than expected
agent_reply: I changed the upload path to behave like a real upload session instead of reopening/appending/syncing the SD file on every network chunk.
What changed:

- `AsyncFsManager` now has `openFileForUpload()`, `writeUploadFileChunk()`, `closeUploadFile()`, and upload session IDs in [AsyncFsManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp).

- `/file_upload` now opens once on the first chunk, writes chunks sequentially by offset, verifies the final raw-body byte count, then syncs/closes once at finish in [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp).
- Client disconnect cleanup now only closes the matching upload session, so a late disconnect cannot kill a newer upload.
- Web UI upload timeout went from 2 minutes to 10 minutes in [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js).
- `inc/web_assets.h` was regenerated by the build.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: success.
This should fix the “partial file then 500” behavior if it was caused by per-chunk SD sync/open/close overhead or browser timeout.

user_prompt: the first file, 5MB, was successful, the second and third both failed and not even created on the card, the dialog says "File write failed" but interestingly the progress bar is 100%
agent_reply: Yep, that 100% progress bar is only “browser finished sending bytes to the ESP,” not “ESP finished committing to SD.” The old message hid whether the failure was open/write/close.
I tightened this up:
- Raw uploads now preallocate the file when `Content-Length` is known.
- Uploads sync every 512 KB instead of relying on one big final flush.
- Final close now truncates preallocated size to actual bytes and checks `close()`.
- `removeFile()` no longer blindly closes whatever upload is active.
- UI/server errors are now more specific: `File open failed`, `File chunk write failed`, `File close failed`, or `Incomplete file upload`.
- `inc/web_assets.h` regenerated from the updated web JS.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: success.
If it still fails, the new dialog text plus serial log SD error codes should tell us exactly which phase is breaking.

user_prompt: hmm now it's `W (82663) AsyncFsManager: open upload failed: could not open path=/splash.af sd_error=0x0C sd_data=0x04`
this is a bit too random
are you sure it's not because of the GUI draws and SPI sharing?
agent_reply: Nope, I’m not sure. In fact this new error makes GUI/SPI sharing look **more** suspicious, not less.

`sd_error=0x0C` is SdFat `CMD18`: **read multiple blocks**. That happened during `g_upload_file.open(path, O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC)`, before the upload is actually writing. So the card failed while SdFat was reading FAT/directory metadata. `sd_data=0x04` is also weird for CMD18, since CMD18 is a valid SD command; that can happen when the command stream gets corrupted or the card is in a confused state.

The current protection is incomplete: [AsyncFsManager.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:101>) makes `guiShouldYield()` true only when `g_upload_file.isOpen()`. But your failure is in [openFileForUpload](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:337>), before `g_upload_file.open()` succeeds. So GUI drawing can still be happening during the exact vulnerable `open()` call.

Also, SD is initialized as shared SPI at 20 MHz first in [MicroSdCard.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:19>) / [MicroSdCard.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:163>). `SHARED_SPI` helps SdFat use transactions correctly, but it is not necessarily a full cross-task display-vs-SD mutex.
My read: likely suspects are, in order:
1. GUI/display SPI activity during SD open/write/sync/close.
2. 20 MHz SD SPI being marginal under display/Wi-Fi load.
3. SD card/power/card-state sensitivity.
4. Some other direct SD access bypassing `AsyncFsManager`.

Best next diagnostic: force SD to `4 MHz` or `10 MHz` and repeat uploads. If the randomness goes away, this is almost certainly bus timing/contention. Then I’d patch `AsyncFsManager` so “upload active” is true before `file.open()` and stays true through close/cancel, causing GUI poll to yield for the whole upload lifecycle.

But your failure is in [openFileForUpload](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:337>), before `g_upload_file.open()` succeeds. So GUI drawing can still be happening during the exact vulnerable `open()` call.

Also, SD is initialized as shared SPI at 20 MHz first in [MicroSdCard.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:19>) / [MicroSdCard.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:163>). `SHARED_SPI` helps SdFat use transactions correctly, but it is not necessarily a full cross-task display-vs-SD mutex.
My read: likely suspects are, in order:
1. GUI/display SPI activity during SD open/write/sync/close.
2. 20 MHz SD SPI being marginal under display/Wi-Fi load.
3. SD card/power/card-state sensitivity.
4. Some other direct SD access bypassing `AsyncFsManager`.

Best next diagnostic: force SD to `4 MHz` or `10 MHz` and repeat uploads. If the randomness goes away, this is almost certainly bus timing/contention. Then I’d patch `AsyncFsManager` so “upload active” is true before `file.open()` and stays true through close/cancel, causing GUI poll to yield for the whole upload lifecycle.

user_prompt: I have added `g_last_file_activity_time = millis();` to a bunch of places, add more, and also zero it out where appropriate
agent_reply: Done in [AsyncFsManager.cpp](<D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:106>).

I added a `note_file_activity()` helper, wired it into the missing SD-touching paths, and made `guiShouldYield()` clear `g_last_file_activity_time` once the 200 ms quiet window expires. `init()` also resets it to `0` after closing everything. Normal close paths keep the short grace period so the GUI does not immediately resume drawing on top of SD activity.
Covered more activity around walk/reset, download open/read/close/reopen, upload open/preallocate/write/sync/truncate/close/cancel, one-shot writes, remove, and `isFile()`.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: build passed. PlatformIO also regenerated `inc/web_assets.h` during the build.

user_prompt: hmmm I am trying to upload a file and I got `W (45542) AsyncFsManager: write file failed: path=/firmware.bin wanted=1436 wrote=0 sync_or_write_failed` and then `W (52681) AsyncFsManager: remove file failed: fs remove failed path=/firmware.bin`
I thought we recently fixed all the upload bugs
agent_reply: Yeah, this was a different upload bug class. The earlier fix covered web file operations colliding with each other; this one was the GUI still being allowed to draw on the shared SPI bus between HTTP upload chunks.
I changed:
- [AsyncFsManager.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp): tracks an active web upload and makes `guiShouldYield()` return true during the whole upload.
- [WebFileHandlers.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp): marks upload active on first chunk, clears it on success/error/disconnect.
- Added SdFat `sd_error` / `sd_data` to write/remove failure logs, so if it still fails we’ll get the real low-level clue.
The `remove file failed` was probably cleanup fallout after the write failed, not the primary failure.
Verification:
- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed.
- `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passed.
- Plain `pio run` still fails because your current `inc/conf.h` requires `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` and the default env doesn’t define it.

user_prompt: Can you also make sure that the GUI isn't in an update? in `void FlyGui::poll()`, right before the call to `M5.update();`, mark the GUI as being busy, up to the end of the redraw call. The stuff in AsyncFsManager can wait on it internally. There isn't much that can happen in the GUI when Wi-Fi is on anyways.
agent_reply: Done. I bracketed `FlyGui::poll()` from just before `M5.update()` through `redraw(false)` with `AsyncFsManager::beginGuiUpdate()` / `endGuiUpdate()` in [FlyGui.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:143).

`AsyncFsManager` now tracks GUI-update ownership and makes filesystem operations wait while another task owns the GUI update. It skips the wait for the GUI task itself, so touch/button callbacks won’t deadlock if they indirectly touch SD.
Verified:
- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed
- `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passed
No build processes left running.

commit_message: web file list exposes file size

user_prompt: see the previous git commit, did I do anything wrong in the merge resolution?
agent_reply: Yes, I found two likely merge-resolution mistakes.
**Findings**

- High: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:557) registers `request->onDisconnect([] { endWebUpload(); })`, then [line 586](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:586) registers another `onDisconnect` for `cancelUploadFile(session_id)`. In this ESPAsyncWebServer, `onDisconnect()` is a setter, so the second callback overwrites the first. If an upload disconnects after the file is opened, `g_web_upload_active` can stay true forever, and [guiShouldYield](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:115) will keep the GUI yielding. Fix by using one combined callback that cancels the upload and ends web upload.

- Medium: [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:45) kept `<a class="delete-link" href="/delete_file?file_name=">`, but [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:249) treats it like a button: sets `disabled`, assigns `onclick`, and does a `fetch(POST)` without `preventDefault()`. Clicking can still follow the stale empty `href`, potentially navigating away or racing/canceling the POST. Best fix is to restore these to `<button type="button" class="delete-link">`, matching the JS side.
**Other Checks**

No unresolved conflict markers found. `node --check html/script.js` passes. `git diff --check HEAD^1 HEAD` reports trailing whitespace in `lib/ESP-FTP-Server-Lib/src/FTPCommand.h`, but that looks like imported/vendor code rather than the conflict area.
I did not change files; this was review-only.
Yes, I found two likely merge-resolution mistakes.
**Findings**

- High: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:557) registers `request->onDisconnect([] { endWebUpload(); })`, then [line 586](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:586) registers another `onDisconnect` for `cancelUploadFile(session_id)`. In this ESPAsyncWebServer, `onDisconnect()` is a setter, so the second callback overwrites the first. If an upload disconnects after the file is opened, `g_web_upload_active` can stay true forever, and [guiShouldYield](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:115) will keep the GUI yielding. Fix by using one combined callback that cancels the upload and ends web upload.

- Medium: [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:45) kept `<a class="delete-link" href="/delete_file?file_name=">`, but [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:249) treats it like a button: sets `disabled`, assigns `onclick`, and does a `fetch(POST)` without `preventDefault()`. Clicking can still follow the stale empty `href`, potentially navigating away or racing/canceling the POST. Best fix is to restore these to `<button type="button" class="delete-link">`, matching the JS side.
**Other Checks**

No unresolved conflict markers found. `node --check html/script.js` passes. `git diff --check HEAD^1 HEAD` reports trailing whitespace in `lib/ESP-FTP-Server-Lib/src/FTPCommand.h`, but that looks like imported/vendor code rather than the conflict area.
I did not change files; this was review-only.

commit_message: merge conflict resolution artifact, too many note for file access
